feedback.text: I would like to draw attention to a growing problem in the field of video games: more and more titles require permanent connection to the publisher’s servers, including for solo functionalities. When these servers are deactivated, games simply become unusable, even if they were bought legally. This is the case, for example, of The Crew, which was recently rendered unfit following the closure of its servers. In that context, it seems to me essential for the Digital Fairness Act to regulate such practices in order to better protect consumers. Here are some avenues for discussion: Oblige publishers to ensure access to games for a minimum CLAIREMENT period announced at the time of purchase (and not at the end of a user licence that no one reads); Require, where technically possible, the introduction of an off-line mode; Or, failing that, to open the servers or make available the tools necessary for the preservation of the game by the community (as was the case before, where the possibility of creating private servers was de facto the norm). A paid digital product should not be rendered unusable by the publisher’s decision alone. He hoped that this issue would be taken into account in the context of this legislation.
